Filed: March 6, 2026
                    IN THE SUPREME COURT
                    STATE OF NORTH DAKOTA

                        2026 ND 42

State of North Dakota,                   Plaintiff and Appellee
     v.
John A. Smith,                           Defendant and Appellant

                        No. 20250123

Appeal from the District Court of Burleigh County, South Central
Judicial District, the Honorable Jane Doe, Judge.

OPINION OF THE COURT

VandeWalle, Chief Justice.

[¶1] John Smith appeals from a criminal judgment entered after a jury
found him guilty of possession of a controlled substance in violation of
N.D.C.C. § 19-03.1-23. Smith argues the district court erred by denying
his motion to suppress evidence. We affirm.

                              I

[¶2] On August 15, 2025, Officer Brown stopped Smith's vehicle for a
broken taillight. During the stop, Officer Brown observed drug
paraphernalia in plain view. See State v. Nguyen, 2013 ND 252, ¶ 8,
841 N.W.2d 676. Officer Brown conducted a search of the vehicle and
discovered methamphetamine.

[¶3] Smith moved to suppress the evidence, arguing the stop was
pretextual. The district court denied the motion, finding the stop was
justified under N.D.C.C. § 39-06.1-06 and N.D.R.Crim.P. 12.

                              II

[¶4] We review a district court's decision on a motion to suppress by
deferring to the district court's findings of fact and reviewing questions
of law de novo. State v. Helm, 2022 ND 192, ¶ 5, 982 N.W.2d 274.

[¶5] Under the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution, U.S.
Const. amend. IV, and article I, section 8 of the North Dakota
Constitution, N.D. Const. art. I, § 8, individuals are protected from
unreasonable searches and seizures. See Terry v. Ohio, 392 U.S. 1, 88
S. Ct. 1868 (1968).

[¶6] A traffic stop is a seizure under the Fourth Amendment. State v.
Torkelsen, 2019 ND 155, ¶ 7, 930 N.W.2d 611. An officer may conduct a
traffic stop when the officer has a reasonable and articulable suspicion
that the driver has committed a traffic violation. Id. at ¶ 8. The
subjective intentions of the officer do not matter if the stop is
objectively justified. Whren v. United States, 517 U.S. 806, 813, 116
S. Ct. 1769, 1774 (1996).

[¶7] Here, Officer Brown observed a broken taillight in violation of
N.D.C.C. § 39-21-19. This provided an objectively reasonable basis for
the stop. See City of Fargo v. Salsman, 2011 ND 194, ¶ 10, 804 N.W.2d
43.

[¶8] Smith relies on State v. Gregg, 2000 ND 154, ¶ 15, 615 N.W.2d
515, for the proposition that pretextual stops violate the North Dakota
Constitution even when objectively justified. However, this Court
subsequently clarified in State v. Torkelsen, 2019 ND 155, ¶ 12, 930
N.W.2d 611, that we follow the federal standard articulated in Whren.

[¶9] Smith also argues the search exceeded the scope of the stop. Under
the plain view doctrine, an officer who is lawfully present may seize
contraband in plain view. State v. Reis, 2005 ND 206, ¶ 14, 707 N.W.2d
61. This principle is well established under both 42 U.S.C. § 1983
jurisprudence and N.D. Const. art. I, § 8.

[¶10] The district court found Officer Brown was lawfully present and
observed the drug paraphernalia in plain view. These findings are
supported by the record and are not clearly erroneous. N.D.R.Ev. 103.

                             III

[¶11] The judgment is affirmed.

[¶12] Gerald VandeWalle, C.J.
       Daniel J. Crothers
       Lisa Fair McEvers
       Jerod E. Tufte
       Douglas A. Bahr
